title: Abraham Sees that Yahweh Sees: A Cohesive Conclusion
speaker: Elder Scott Hollander
source_files: Abraham Sees that Yahweh Sees - A Cohesive Conclusion - Elder Scott Hollander - Genesis 22_1-19.txt

outline.title: The Setting: Mount Moriah and Its Significance
subpoints: The sacrifice of Isaac takes place on one of the mountains of Moriah, named only twice in Scripture: here and in 2 Chronicles 3:1 where Solomon built the temple
2 Samuel 24:16-25 records that David purchased the threshing floor on this same site, built an altar, and offered burnt offerings to remove God's discipline
Topographic maps show Mount Moriah in Jerusalem adjacent to the location scholars identify as Golgotha — the place of the crucifixion
The Church of the Holy Sepulchre, believed to mark both Golgotha and the tomb, sits outside the ancient city walls — fulfilling the requirement of Genesis 22:14
All of these high points in the region technically constitute the mountains of Moriah, so the whole region ties together prophetically

outline.title: Isaac as a Type of Christ: A Chart of Parallels
subpoints: Miraculous birth: Isaac born to a menopausal Sarah foreshadows the miraculous nature of Christ's birth
Only son sacrificed on a mountain of Moriah; figuratively dead for three days and figuratively resurrected (Hebrews 11:17-19)
Accompanied to his death by two servants; compelled to carry his own sacrificial wood upon his back
Isaac willingly submitted to the will of his father — and could have escaped, being younger and stronger than the elderly Abraham
Through the obedience of Abraham and Isaac, all the nations of the earth would be blessed — fulfilled ultimately in Christ (1 John 2:2)

outline.title: The Theme: Abraham Sees that Yahweh Sees
subpoints: Nine repeated interjections and verbs throughout the passage all relate to seeing — the dominant theme of the pericope
The Hebrew root *ra'ah* (to see) appears in verses 4, 8, 13, and 14; the interjection *hinneh* (behold/look/here I am) appears in verses 1, 7, and 11
Yahweh Yireh (commonly Jehovah Jireh) literally means 'Yahweh he will see' — not 'Yahweh provides' in a supply sense
The Latin etymology of 'provide' (pro + videre = to see ahead) and the English word 'video' (from Latin 'I see') both trace back to seeing — not supplying
God provides by seeing and foreknowing; his foresight leads to action; providence and provision are rooted in the divine act of seeing

outline.title: Jesus Chose the Word 'Saw' to Point to Genesis 22
subpoints: John 8:56: 'Your father Abraham rejoiced that he would see my day; he saw it and was glad' — Jesus deliberately uses seeing language
Jesus could have said 'Abraham rejoiced that my day would come and he was glad' — removing all sight vocabulary — but he did not
Elder Hollander argues Jesus intentionally echoed the Genesis 22 theme of Abraham seeing that Yahweh sees
Fourth-to-ninth-century Syriac church fathers also made this connection, so the reading is not idiosyncratic
The connection is not just a vision but Abraham's understanding through the typological events of Genesis 22

outline.title: The Abrahamic Covenant Re-expressed and Expanded
subpoints: Tautological infinitives in verses 17-18: 'blessing I will bless you, and multiplying I will multiply you' — emphatic certainty, best translated 'I will surely bless and multiply'
The stars-of-heaven simile first appears in Genesis 15:5; it is repeated here with the sand-of-the-seashore simile added — an expansion of the original promise
New addition to the covenant: 'your descendants shall possess the gate of their enemies' — later echoed in the blessing of Rebekah (Genesis 24:60)
The 'in your seed all nations shall be blessed' phrase is not new but is now fully clarified to point toward Christ's universal atonement
1 John 2:2: 'He is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world' — the fulfillment of this covenant promise

outline.title: Internal Repetitions: Narrative Threading
subpoints: The phrase 'your son, your only son' appears in verse 2 and is repeated in verses 12 and 16 — emphasizing the significance and foreshadowing New Testament language for Christ
The word 'take' in verse 2 (take your son) is echoed in verse 6 (took the wood, took the fire) — showing Abraham obeying the precise command he was given
'The two of them went together' appears in verse 6 and verbatim again in verse 8, then is brought to completion in verse 19 as 'they went together'
Abraham's statement 'the lad and I will go yonder and worship and we will come back to you' (v. 5) threads through the narrative as a statement of faith in resurrection
The narrative slows in verses 9-10 as each step of the sacrifice is captured, building tension toward the climactic moment of the raised knife

summary:
In this second part of a two-session study on Genesis 22, Elder Scott Hollander delivers a structured and textually rich examination of the themes, typology, and covenant context of Abraham's binding of Isaac. The lesson opens with a geographical argument: the mountains of Moriah, where Abraham offered Isaac, are the same region where Solomon built the temple (2 Chronicles 3:1), where David offered sacrifices at Araunah's threshing floor (2 Samuel 24), and where scholars believe Jesus was crucified — Golgotha, now inside the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, outside the ancient city walls.

The core argument of the lesson is that Genesis 22 presents Isaac as a type of Christ in remarkable detail: miraculous birth, only son, three-day figurative death and resurrection (Hebrews 11:17-19), carrying his own sacrificial wood, willingly submitting to the will of his father, and becoming the means by which all nations are blessed. Elder Hollander demonstrates through a Hebrew vocabulary chart that the passage is dominated by nine instances of seeing vocabulary — the root *ra'ah* and the interjection *hinneh* — establishing the theme 'Abraham sees that Yahweh sees.'

The title Yahweh Yireh (Genesis 22:14), commonly rendered Jehovah Jireh and translated 'the LORD provides,' is shown to mean literally 'Yahweh he will see.' The English words 'video' and 'provide' both trace to the Latin root for seeing (*videre*), demonstrating that to provide is to see ahead and act on what one sees. God's providence and provision are rooted in his divine foresight and foreordination.

The lesson concludes with analysis of the re-expression of the Abrahamic covenant in verses 17-18, including newly added promises (the seashore simile, possession of the enemies' gate), and with close attention to the internal repetitions of 'only son,' 'took,' and 'went together' that thread through the narrative with theological significance.
transcript:
## Introduction and Apology

We are essentially continuing on from last week's lesson. The slide on the screen has the same purpose and application points from last week. Since they remain unchanged, I will leave that there briefly while I offer an apology.

How many of you have ever played the game Mad Libs? Basically, in Mad Libs, there is a short story with blanks within the storyline where the reader prompts other players for certain types of words — an adjective, an adverb, verb, noun. The players have no context to the story, so they supply words at random. The fun lies in reading the completed story with all the strange words filled in.

That is how I felt about last week's lesson. I had done significant research and was confident I knew the material well. However, I suffer from a condition that means it is difficult for me to find words when I need them — even regular, uncomplicated words just escape me while I am standing up here. So when I thought I could ad lib my presentation, it turned into Mad Lib. For that I apologize.

The lesson learned: I spent more time this week organizing my thoughts. This is part two, and I hope it will be a blessing. I am genuinely fascinated with these studies — which is why I bring them to you, and then I disappoint myself when I cannot communicate the excitement I feel. So let us get into it.

I forgot my Bible, but there is one here. Turn with me to Genesis 22. I will read verses 1 through 19 to re-familiarize us with the passage.

## Scripture Reading: Genesis 22:1-19

*Now it came to pass after these things that God tested Abraham, and said to him, 'Abraham!' And he said, 'Here I am.' Then He said, 'Take now your son, your only son Isaac, whom you love, and go to the land of Moriah, and offer him there as a burnt offering on one of the mountains of which I shall tell you.' So Abraham rose early in the morning and saddled his donkey, and took two of his young men with him, and Isaac his son; and he split the wood for the burnt offering, and arose and went to the place of which God had told him. Then on the third day Abraham lifted his eyes and saw the place afar off. And Abraham said to his young men, 'Stay here with the donkey; the lad and I will go yonder and worship, and we will come back to you.' So Abraham took the wood of the burnt offering and laid it on Isaac his son; and he took the fire in his hand, and a knife, and the two of them went together. But Isaac spoke to Abraham his father and said, 'My father!' And he said, 'Here I am, my son.' Then he said, 'Look, the fire and the wood, but where is the lamb for a burnt offering?' And Abraham said, 'My son, God will provide for Himself the lamb for a burnt offering.' So the two of them went together. Then they came to the place of which God had told him. And Abraham built an altar there and placed the wood in order; and he bound Isaac his son and laid him on the altar, upon the wood. And Abraham stretched out his hand and took the knife to slay his son. But the Angel of the LORD called to him from heaven and said, 'Abraham, Abraham!' So he said, 'Here I am.' And He said, 'Do not lay your hand on the lad, or do anything to him; for now I know that you fear God, since you have not withheld your son, your only son, from Me.' Then Abraham lifted his eyes and looked, and there behind him was a ram caught in a thicket by its horns. So Abraham went and took the ram, and offered it up for a burnt offering instead of his son. And Abraham called the name of the place The-LORD-Will-Provide; as it is said to this day, 'In the Mount of the LORD it shall be provided.' Then the Angel of the LORD called to Abraham a second time out of heaven, and said: 'By Myself I have sworn, says the LORD, because you have done this thing, and have not withheld your son, your only son — blessing I will bless you, and multiplying I will multiply your descendants as the stars of the heaven and as the sand which is on the seashore; and your descendants shall possess the gate of their enemies. In your seed all the nations of the earth shall be blessed, because you have obeyed My voice.' So Abraham returned to his young men, and they rose and went together to Beersheba; and Abraham dwelt at Beersheba.*

And thus ends the reading of God's Word.

## The Setting: Mount Moriah and Its Geographical Significance

The setting of Genesis 22 is important, and I wanted to take a moment to consider how it points forward to Jesus Christ. This testing of Abraham — the sacrifice of Isaac — takes place on a mountain in the land of Moriah that God will show to Abraham.

Apart from this passage, the mountain of Moriah is named only once — in 2 Chronicles 3:1, where Solomon built the temple in Jerusalem. And 2 Samuel 24:16-25, though it does not name the site as Moriah specifically, describes the same place: where Yahweh visited David after the unlawful census, where David purchased the threshing floor, built an altar, and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings to remove the plague of discipline from the Israelites.

On the screen I have maps of Mount Moriah in Jerusalem, showing it in comparison to the surrounding territory and to the hill upon which scholars believe Jesus was crucified — which would be in fulfillment of Genesis 22:14. Technically all these high points represent the mountains of Moriah. Most scholars believe the site now called the Church of the Holy Sepulchre contains the locations where Jesus was crucified at Golgotha and buried in a tomb only meters away. This location meets the requirement of Golgotha being outside the city limits — you can see the adjacent city walls on both maps. That is Golgotha.

So the same region, the same mountains: the sacrifice of Isaac, the temple of Solomon, the threshing floor of David, the crucifixion of Christ.

## Abraham Saw the Day of Christ: Connecting John 8:56 to Genesis 22

Last week we were tying Genesis 22 to John 8:56 — Jesus's statement pointing back to Abraham. Jesus says: *Your father Abraham rejoiced that he would see my day; he saw it and was glad.*

I want to demonstrate the correlation between what Abraham saw and what prefigures Christ — that Abraham saw the day of redemption, the willing sacrifice of Jesus Christ as the atoning Lamb, the far greater sacrifice that would permanently atone for man's sin by the one and only beloved Son who would become the resurrected one and our resurrection hope. And he saw all of this through his son Isaac.

Look at this chart of comparison. The parallels from Genesis 22 to Christ are striking. Miraculous birth — what that must have looked like for a menopausal woman to conceive is genuinely remarkable, and it is a foreshadowing type. Isaac is the only son sacrificed on a mountain of Moriah. He was figuratively dead for three days and figuratively resurrected — Hebrews 11:17-19 explicitly interprets it this way. Isaac was accompanied to his death by two servants. He was compelled to carry his own sacrificial wood upon his back — just as Christ carried the cross. When the moment for the sacrifice came, Isaac willingly submitted to the will of his father. And because of the obedience of Abraham and Isaac, all the nations of the earth would be blessed.

Now, I believe Jesus purposely chose the word *saw* in John 8:56 to point back to the theme of Genesis 22 — which I am calling 'Abraham sees that Yahweh sees.' Consider what Jesus could have said to convey the same thought without using sight language: 'Your father Abraham rejoiced that my day would come and he was glad.' Or: 'Abraham was resolved that the coming Messiah would bring fulfillment and he was glad.' He could have left out the word *saw* entirely. He did not.

## The Theme: Nine Seeing Words in Genesis 22

How do I justify the theme 'Abraham sees that Yahweh sees'? There are nine repeated interjections and verbs throughout this passage that all relate to seeing in one fashion or another.

The simplest seeing words are in verses 4, 8, 13, and 14, all using variations of the Hebrew root *ra'ah*, which means to see. The interjections in verses 1, 7, and 11 — translated 'here I am' in English — come from the Hebrew *hinneh*, which literally means 'behold' or 'look.' When a character makes his presence known with this word, he is essentially saying, *look, I am right here.* So even 'here I am' is seeing language.

Perhaps you grew up singing *Jehovah Jireh* — 'Jehovah Jireh, my provider, his grace is sufficient for me.' That title comes from verse 14. I prefer the pronunciation Yahweh Yireh. And here is what I find fascinating about this title: *Yireh* does not come from a word meaning 'to supply.' It comes from the verb *ra'ah* — to see. Yahweh Yireh literally means: *Yahweh he will see.*

From this we can understand: the Lord who sees provides; the Lord will see to it; the Lord who sees will see to it. But how do we get from 'to see' to the English concept of 'provide'? Here is where it gets interesting.

In 1935 the word *video* became popular as the visual equivalent of *audio*. It comes from Latin *videre* — I see. Similarly, the word *provide* comes from Latin *providere*: *pro* (ahead) + *videre* (to see) = I foresee, I act with foresight, I provide. And from this we also get *providens* — Providence.

The Lord acts with foresight and foreknowledge. The Lord sees, and he sees to it. He sees our needs and takes action to care for us and supply for us. We should see and acknowledge the hand of Providence all around us. And the biblical use of foreknowledge and Providence is not merely prescience — not God reacting to what he knew would happen. In his providence he determines ahead of time the plan and actions he will bring to fruition. He sees, he knows, he acts.

## Questions and Conclusions on the Sacrifice

At this point we had some discussion about the passage. One question raised: the figurative sacrifice and resurrection in Hebrews 11 — both are described as figurative, meaning the author of Hebrews interprets the events of Genesis 22 typologically rather than literally applying resurrection to Isaac.

Another question: in verse 8, when Abraham says 'God will provide for himself the lamb,' did he know it was prophetic? The answer is probably no on the way up — he was doing everything necessary to kill Isaac, which is why Hebrews 11 says he believed God would raise Isaac from the dead. But on the way down, after the ram was provided, he named the place Yahweh Yireh and declared the proverb 'in the mount of the LORD it shall be provided' — and at that point he did understand that there was something profoundly providential happening that pointed beyond that moment.

Isaac's willing submission is also noteworthy. Whatever his age — and he could have been as young as a teen or as old as thirty-seven — he was certainly faster and stronger than the elderly Abraham. He went willingly. He asked one question, received an answer, and trusted his father to the end.

## The Abrahamic Covenant Re-expressed and Expanded

I want to cover one more topic in our remaining time — the strange language of verse 17 and the re-expression of the Abrahamic covenant.

Our English text reads: *blessing I will bless you, and multiplying I will multiply.* That is awkward in English. What we have here are tautological infinitives — redundant verbs used to mark the speaker's strength of assertion. It could easily be translated: *I will surely bless you and I will surely multiply you.* The redundancy demonstrates the certainty of God's promise. These verbs also have a factitive sense — the act of God's blessing and multiplying causes Abraham to be in the state of being blessed and multiplied.

Now, the covenant language. The original proclamation of the Abrahamic covenant is in Genesis 12. Since then it has been repeated and clarified in Genesis 15, 17, 20, and 21, and now here in 22. The text says: *I will multiply your descendants as the stars of the heaven and as the sand which is on the seashore.* The stars-of-heaven comparison was first added in Genesis 15, where God brought Abraham outside and said, 'Count the stars if you are able — so shall your descendants be, and Abraham believed the LORD, and he counted it to him as righteousness.' But here in Genesis 22, Yahweh expands the illustration to include the sand on the seashore — a new addition. This addition is later repeated by Jacob in Genesis 32:12, where Jacob prays for deliverance from Esau, essentially saying: God, you promised to multiply my descendants — you cannot do that if Esau kills us.

The promise 'your descendants shall possess the gate of their enemies' is also new to the Abrahamic blessing here. But it is later reinforced in the blessing of Rebekah: Genesis 24:60 — *'Our sister, may you become the mother of thousands of ten thousands; and may your descendants possess the gate of those who hate them.'*

And the final phrase, *in your seed all the nations of the earth shall be blessed*, is not new to the Abrahamic covenant — but as we have demonstrated, this passage points to fulfillment in Christ. This promise is ultimately satisfied in Jesus Christ, as noted by the Apostle John in 1 John 2:2: *He is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world.* Not just a blessing for Abraham and his family, or for the Israelite nation, but the whole world.

## Internal Repetitions: Narrative Threading

There are two noteworthy internal repetitions from Genesis 22:2 that are worth observing.

First: 'your son, your only son.' This phrase from verse 2 is repeated in verses 12 and 16. The redundancy emphasizes the significance of Abraham's sacrifice in relation to his beloved son, and it also foreshadows the New Testament language for Christ as the one and only Son.

Second: the word *take*. It appears in the original command of verse 2 and is then echoed in verse 6 — *Abraham took the wood of the burnt offering and laid it on Isaac his son; and he took the fire in his hand.* Twice in one sentence. The author is quietly demonstrating that Abraham is obeying the precise command he was given: 'Take your son' — and he took his son and took the materials for the sacrifice.

And the phrase *the two of them went together* appears in verse 6 and verbatim again in verse 8, and then is brought to completion in verse 19: *So Abraham returned to his young men, and they rose and went together.* The narrative threads this phrase through as a subtle note of the journey Abraham and Isaac shared — and the journey they completed together.

I believe we have completed the mission to prove the points I set out at the beginning. The theme of Genesis 22 is: Abraham sees that Yahweh sees. And I believe Jesus intentionally chose the word *saw* in John 8:56 to point back to that theme — that Abraham, through the events of Genesis 22, saw the day of Christ.

Are there any remaining questions?

*[Discussion and prayer followed.]*

Heavenly Father, we are grateful for this time of study. I pray that it was a blessing. We are grateful that your Word does not return void, that it is always proven true and factual, and that it happened in time and space so that we can look back and appreciate it. I pray that it would encourage us to follow hard after you, trusting in Jesus Christ our Savior and resting in the fact that you are true to your promises. We ask this in Christ's name. Amen.
